message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To control COVID-19 outbreak in Japan, sports and entertainment events were canceled in Japan for two weeks from 26 February to 11 March. It has been designated as voluntary event cancellation (VEC).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Object</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study predicts the effectiveness of VEC enduring and after its implementation.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We applied a simple susceptible–infected–recovery model to data of patients with symptoms in Japan during 14 January to VEC introduction and after VEC introduction to 8 March. We adjusted the reporting delay in the latest few days.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Results suggest that the basic reproduction number, R
                    <jats:sub>0,</jats:sub>
                    before VEC introduced as 2.50 with a 95% confidence interval (CI) was [2.43, 2.55] and the effective reproduction number, R
                    <jats:sub>v,</jats:sub>
                    after VEC introduced as 1. 88; its 95% CI was [1.68,2.02].
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Discussion and Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Results demonstrated that VEC can reduce COVID-19 infectiousness by 35%, but R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    remains higher than one.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Preliminary evaluation of voluntary event cancellation as a countermeasure against the COVID-19 outbreak in Japan as of 11 March, 2020
